_readme.what: Routing-oriented model knowledge base for AI agents and the Model Intelligence panel. The panel is a typeset rendering of THIS file — same ids, same values. As an agent, read this whole object before routing: pick a model by matching a task against each model's bestFor (and not avoidFor), then break ties by price (price.inputPerM/outputPerM, USD per million tokens — lower is cheaper), speed (speedTps, output tokens/sec — higher is faster/lower-latency), context (max input window in tokens — bigger fits more code/docs), and benchmarks (benchmarks.aaIndex is the headline intelligence score, higher = smarter; sweBench/lmarenaElo for coding).
_readme.howAgentsUse: 1) Filter models to roster.inPlay===true (the user's hard gate — never route to an out-of-roster or status==='fading' model). 2) Match the task to bestFor and exclude any avoidFor. 3) Use tier ('frontier' = top intelligence, 'fast' = low-latency cheap, 'open' = open-weight value, 'specialist' = task-tuned) and the `routing` rules to choose. 4) Break ties with price/speedTps/context/benchmarks. Quote `freshness.snapshot.curatedAsOf` when citing sentiment/benchmarks; price/context/speedTps may be fresher (see `freshness.live`). Consult `picks` for fast canned routing (smartest / proFavorite / fastest / cheapest / rising).
_readme.fieldProvenance: Every drift-prone field is tagged via top-level `freshness.liveFields` (overwritten from OpenRouter at render) vs everything else (curated, dated to freshness.snapshot.curatedAsOf). Per-model `liveFields` records what actually merged this render; nulls fall back to curated values.
_readme.humanControl: roster.inPlay is the ONLY user-authored field (localStorage key claude-os-model-roster). It is the agent's hard gate — do not route where inPlay===false.

rules: Hardest reasoning / multi-file refactor → highest benchmarks.aaIndex frontier model with roster.inPlay===true.
High-volume or latency-critical → cheapest in-roster model with speedTps>=80 and tier in ['fast','open'].
Never route where roster.inPlay===false or status==='fading'.

picks.label: Smartest
picks.reason: #1 on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index (61) and the runaway practitioner default for production work.

picks.label: Pros reach for
picks.reason: Best long-horizon agentic coding and reliability; ~4x less likely than 4.7 to let its own code flaws pass.

picks.label: Fastest
picks.reason: Near-fastest output (280+ t/s) with Index 55 — best speed/intelligence blend of the fast tier.

picks.label: Best value
picks.reason: #1 model on OpenRouter by volume at just $0.10/$0.20 per M with 1M context — the cheapest credible agentic brain.

picks.label: Up & coming
picks.reason: Best intelligence-per-dollar on the AA leaderboard; #3 overall and #2 in coding on OpenRouter within a week of launch.

bestOverall.reason: #1 on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index (61) and the runaway practitioner default for production work.

bestCoding.reason: #1 on LMArena WebDev/Code (1567) and the Terminal-Bench 2.0 champion (90.2%); Opus 4.8 is its close, newer sibling.

bestCheapFast.reason: #1 model on OpenRouter by volume at just $0.10/$0.20 per M with 1M context — the cheapest credible agentic brain.

bestLongContext.reason: Largest context window of the frontier set at 2,000,000 tokens, at cheap-output pricing.

bestOpenWeight.reason: The only non-Western model rated 'Tier-A with no caveats' — uniquely stable over 4,000+ tool calls / 13-hour agent runs.

bestForAgents.reason: Best long-horizon agentic coding and reliability; ~4x less likely than 4.7 to let its own code flaws pass.

models.name: Claude Opus 4.8
models.oneLiner: Anthropic's flagship and the practitioner default for production and long-horizon agentic coding.

sentiment.label: very positive
sentiment.summary: The runaway practitioner favorite for production and agentic coding; the gripes are price and a cautious tone, not capability.
loved: Best agentic/SWE coding and long-context reliability on monorepos and long agent runs
Tops the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index (61)
Favorite inside Cursor and Claude Code; 'textbook-correct code, impeccable architecture'
~4x less likely than Opus 4.7 to let its own code flaws pass
gripes: Most expensive frontier option at $5/$25 per M
High-effort (xhigh/max) modes burn lots of tokens
More cautious/hedging tone than GPT
Newer tokenizer 'optimizes aggressively, sometimes cutting corners'

weaknesses: Highest token cost of the frontier tier
Slow first token in max-reasoning mode (31s TTFT)
models.proUsage: Reserved as the premium 'hard task' brain — pros route the toughest reasoning/coding to it and fall back to cheaper open models for volume.

models.name: Claude Opus 4.7
models.oneLiner: Prior Anthropic flagship and still the most-used premium coding model in real OpenRouter traffic.

sentiment.label: very positive
sentiment.summary: The proven workhorse of premium coding; #1 on the LMArena WebDev/Code board and the live Terminal-Bench champion.
loved: Highest-ranked premium closed model in real OpenRouter coding usage
gripes: Same premium price as 4.8 with slightly lower top-line intelligence
Superseded by 4.8 for the hardest reasoning

weaknesses: No longer the top intelligence tier
avoidFor: When 4.8's extra reasoning headroom is needed
models.proUsage: Still the default premium coding model in many agent harnesses; pros keep it for proven WebDev/terminal reliability.

models.name: Claude Sonnet 4.6
models.oneLiner: Anthropic's mid/workhorse tier — best speed/intelligence balance and the writing & voice-fidelity specialist.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.summary: The writing and voice-fidelity specialist at a fair price; the 'smart-but-cheap' workhorse pick.
loved: Best-in-class writing style, voice fidelity and literal instruction-following
Highest-ranked Western model by raw OpenRouter token volume (#6 overall)
gripes: Not the strongest pure reasoner
Weaker agentic/tool ceiling than Opus

avoidFor: Hardest reasoning or longest agent runs
models.proUsage: The everyday production driver — pros run it for most coding and writing, escalating to Opus only for the hardest jobs.

models.name: Claude Haiku 4.5
models.oneLiner: Anthropic's fast near-frontier tier for high-volume classification, routing and code review.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.summary: Anthropic's fastest model — the cheap, low-latency option for high-throughput tasks.
loved: Near-frontier quality for a fast tier
gripes: 200K context vs the 1M of the bigger Claude tiers
Not for hard reasoning

models.proUsage: Used as the cheap Claude tier for routing/classification and first-pass code review inside larger pipelines.

models.name: GPT-5.5
models.oneLiner: OpenAI's current flagship and the trusted, lower-hallucination daily driver developers benchmark against.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.summary: The reliable, lower-hallucination daily driver; live SWE-bench Verified champion, dinged on cost and 'incremental' feel.
loved: Top of the Vals.ai SWE-bench Verified board (82.6%)
40% cheaper + 20% faster than 5.4 at similar quality
gripes: Some call it 'GPT-5.4 with a new label'
Less expressive than Sonnet for style
GPT-5.5 Pro at $100/mo is steep; 2x the price of 5.4

models.proUsage: The trusted general-purpose driver — pros use it for reliable coding/agentic work and as the benchmark baseline.

models.name: GPT-5.5 Pro
models.oneLiner: OpenAI's max-reasoning tier — the most expensive model in the catalog, reserved for the hardest problems.

sentiment.label: mixed
sentiment.summary: Maximum-reasoning tier that pros respect but few run at scale — the $30/$180 pricing and $100/mo Pro plan are the sticking point.
loved: Highest reasoning effort tier OpenAI offers
Top scores on the hardest reasoning evals
gripes: Most expensive model in the catalog at $30/$180 per M
$100/mo Pro plan is steep
Overkill for routine work

weaknesses: Slow and overkill for routine tasks
models.proUsage: Pulled out only for the single hardest reasoning problems where cost is no object.

models.name: GPT-5.5 Instant
models.oneLiner: ChatGPT's new default model — a fast workhorse with reduced hallucinations and better personalization.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.summary: The new ChatGPT default for all users — fast and reliable, with the caveat of a trimmed context and older cutoff.
loved: Default model for every ChatGPT user since May 5
gripes: Instant tier trims context to 400K with an older cutoff
Not a deep-reasoning tier

models.proUsage: The mass-market fast default; pros mostly hit GPT-5.5 proper for serious work rather than Instant.

models.name: GPT-5.4 Nano
models.oneLiner: OpenAI's cheapest API tier — the budget GPT for high-volume, simple tasks.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.summary: The cheapest GPT-5.x tier — the go-to OpenAI option when cost matters more than depth.
gripes: Limited reasoning
Outclassed on quality by Chinese open-weight value tiers

weaknesses: Beaten on price/quality by open-weight models
models.proUsage: A cheap OpenAI fallback for simple high-volume calls when staying in the OpenAI ecosystem.

models.name: GPT-5.3 Codex
models.oneLiner: OpenAI's coding-specialized line, tuned for high-effort agentic software tasks.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.summary: OpenAI's dedicated coding model — strong on standardized SWE-bench, with very slow first-token times at high reasoning effort.
gripes: Very high latency at xhigh effort (~103s TTFT)
Mid-pack vs the newest frontier on top-line intelligence

weaknesses: Slow first token at high effort
models.proUsage: Run through the Codex CLI for autonomous software tasks where its coding tuning earns the slow-but-thorough tradeoff.

models.name: Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview
models.oneLiner: Google's available frontier reasoning model — the accuracy/reasoning leader with strong value pricing.

sentiment.label: mixed
sentiment.summary: The accuracy/reasoning champion (GPQA-Diamond and HLE leader) that polarizes builders on coding quality and quotas.
loved: Tops the hardest reasoning tests — GPQA-Diamond 94.1%
Strong value at $2/$12 per M, 143 t/s
gripes: Blunt HN criticism on coding quality vs its reasoning
In-app API quotas and rate limits frustrate heavy users
'Accuracy leader constrained by artificial usage limits'

models.proUsage: Pulled in for the hardest reasoning, multimodal and search-grounded tasks; pros route coding elsewhere.

models.name: Gemini 3.5 Flash
models.oneLiner: Google's speed-leading agentic Flash — 'the Flash that beat last year's Pro', now the flashpoint of the price-hike debate.

sentiment.label: mixed
sentiment.summary: Loved on capability and speed, but its 3x price hike became the year's loudest gripe and the emblem of 'the era of cheap AI is ending'.
loved: 'The Flash that beat last year's Pro'
280+ t/s with Index 55 — best speed/intelligence blend
Free in the Gemini app; Google's pick to power 'everything'
gripes: 3x input price increase ($1.50/$9) over Gemini 3 Flash Preview — Simon Willison + HN made it the loudest thread of spring
Effective cost ~5x higher due to token usage
Some report an 'IQ drop' vs expectations

models.proUsage: The speed pick for agentic and multimodal loops where fast first/last token matters more than rock-bottom cost.

models.name: Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite
models.oneLiner: The cheapest current Gemini — a multimodal value tier with full 1M context.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.summary: The budget Gemini — cheap multimodal access with a full 1M-token window.
loved: Cheapest current Gemini at $0.25/$1.50 per M
gripes: Lightest of the Gemini line on raw capability

models.proUsage: Cheap Gemini tier for high-volume multimodal or long-context jobs that don't need the flagship.

models.name: Grok 4.3
models.oneLiner: xAI's fast, permissive cheap-output frontier model with native real-time X/web grounding.

sentiment.label: mixed
sentiment.summary: Fun, fast and permissive with real-time grounding, but practitioners say it 'still hasn't found a lane that makes economic sense' for coding.
loved: Most permissive guardrails of any frontier model
Big 47-pt generational jump; cheap output at $2.50/M
gripes: Trails Claude/GPT/Gemini for coding utility (treated as backup/for fun)
~$1.74/run for a worse result than cheaper Tier-A
Community felt 'betrayed' by Jan-2026 moderation tightening
$30/mo SuperGrok for full access

models.proUsage: Used mostly as a fast, real-time-grounded backup or for permissive open-ended generation, not primary coding.

models.name: Grok 4.20
models.oneLiner: xAI's largest-context frontier model — a 2M-token window at cheap-output pricing.

sentiment.label: mixed
sentiment.summary: Notable mainly for the largest context of the frontier set (2M tokens); inherits the Grok 4.x coding-economics and moderation caveats.
loved: 2,000,000-token context — largest of the frontier set
gripes: Same coding-utility and moderation caveats as the Grok 4.x line

models.proUsage: Reached for when an unusually large 2M-token context window is the deciding factor.

models.name: DeepSeek V4 Flash
models.oneLiner: The #1 model on OpenRouter by token throughput — the cheapest credible agentic brain with 1M context.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.summary: The go-to budget brain for high-volume/agentic loops — unbeatable cost-to-capability, with the usual DeepSeek tooling lag and weaker hard-reasoning.
loved: #1 model overall on OpenRouter (~19% of weekly volume)
gripes: Noticeably weaker on hardest reasoning
DeepSeek tooling/harness lag (protocol incompatibilities)
Flash much weaker than Pro on hard tasks

strengths: Lowest cost of any credible model
models.proUsage: The default cheap workhorse — pros route high-volume agentic and bulk traffic here and escalate only the hard cases.

models.name: DeepSeek V4 Pro
models.oneLiner: DeepSeek's open-weight frontier value play — near-frontier intelligence at roughly 1/20th the closed-model price.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.summary: Top-tier value — Intelligence Index 52 at ~$0.18/M blended, a top open-weight coding and agentic pick.
loved: Index 52 at just ~$0.18/M blended — top-tier value
1.6T total / 49B active MoE, 1M context, 384K max output
#7 overall and #4 in programming on OpenRouter
gripes: Intelligence Index trails the closed frontier
DeepSeek tooling-lag caveat applies

weaknesses: Below closed frontier on top-line intelligence
models.proUsage: The 'near-frontier for cheap' pick — pros use it when they want strong coding without paying closed-model rates.

models.name: Tencent Hy3 Preview
models.oneLiner: The cheapest of the top OpenRouter tier and #2 model overall by raw token volume.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.summary: A volume juggernaut — #2 model overall on OpenRouter and the cheapest of the top tier, riding raw price-throughput.
loved: #2 model overall on OpenRouter by token throughput
Cheapest of the top tier at $0.063/$0.21 per M
gripes: Preview status
Smaller 256K context than the 1M-class open peers
Thin English-language practitioner coverage

models.proUsage: Used as one of the cheapest possible throughput engines for high-volume token loads.

models.name: Xiaomi MiMo-V2.5
models.oneLiner: #1 in the OpenRouter Programming category — a cheap multimodal open model that owns coding-token volume.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.summary: The coding-volume champion among open models — #1 in OpenRouter's Programming category and excellent intelligence-per-dollar (Pro variant Index 54 at $0.18/M).
loved: MiMo-V2.5-Pro hits Index 54 at $0.18/M — among the cheapest at that intelligence
Very cheap base at $0.14/$0.28 per M
gripes: Below closed frontier on absolute quality
LMArena WebDev rank (Pro #19) trails its raw volume lead

avoidFor: Hardest reasoning where closed frontier wins
models.proUsage: A favored cheap coding engine for high-volume programming workloads where closed-model cost can't be justified.

models.name: MiniMax M3
models.oneLiner: The biggest open-weight release of the quarter — best intelligence-per-dollar, claiming frontier coding at ~5-10% of the cost.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.summary: Huge value buzz — best intelligence-per-dollar on the AA leaderboard, already #3 overall and #2 in coding on OpenRouter; code-auditors stay a bit skeptical.
loved: #3 overall and #2 in programming on OpenRouter within a week of launch
59.0% SWE-Bench Pro; claims edge over GPT-5.5/Gemini 3.1 Pro on coding
gripes: Some audits ding the code ('invents a batch signature that crashes')
Unproven vendor stability
Earlier MiniMax versions had restrictive licensing

avoidFor: When audited code-correctness guarantees matter most
models.proUsage: The buzzy value pick — pros run it for near-frontier coding at a fraction of closed-model cost, auditing output on critical paths.

models.name: Kimi K2.6
models.oneLiner: The open-weight darling — 'cheapest Tier-A, no caveats', uniquely stable over very long agentic sessions.

sentiment.label: very positive
sentiment.summary: The most-loved open-weight model in practitioner threads — the only non-Western model rated Tier-A 'with no caveats', prized for marathon agentic stability.
loved: Rated 'Tier-A with no caveats'; leading open-weights frontier (Index 54)
Best-in-class long-session stability (4,000+ tool calls over a 13-hour unbroken run)
Ties GPT-5.5 on SWE-Bench Pro (58.6%), leads HLE-with-tools (54.0%)
gripes: Limited English docs
Smaller global community
Fragmented API providers
256K context vs 1M-class peers

bestFor: Very long agentic sessions / agent swarms
avoidFor: Teams needing rich English tooling docs
models.proUsage: The open-weight pick for long-running agent swarms — pros lean on its 4,000+ tool-call stability for marathon coding runs.

models.name: Qwen3.7 Max
models.oneLiner: China's top closed-weight flagship — an agent-first reasoning model matching GPT-5.5 medium at low latency.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.summary: Strong mid-tier value and the top non-Anthropic WebDev coder, with trust/local-toolchain friction; flagship is API-only.
loved: Index 57 matching GPT-5.5 medium, very low 2.6s latency, $1.43/M blended
gripes: Flagship is API-only (no chat UI)
Unconfirmed long-term pricing/vendor-trust questions
Local Qwen toolchain pain (llama.cpp bugs, missing tool-call parsers)

models.proUsage: Used as a strong, low-latency agentic coder — the top non-Claude WebDev option for pros who can use the API.

models.name: GLM-5.1
models.oneLiner: A strong-on-paper open coder that became the quarter's surprise regression in independent re-audits.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.summary: The surprise regression after a well-liked GLM-5 — strong benchmark numbers (LMArena WebDev #8) undercut by structural bugs found in practitioner re-audits.
loved: MIT license; marketed for 8-hour autonomous coding runs
gripes: 'Most painful fall from the re-audit' — dropped from Tier B to Tier C
Structural bugs ('invented fluent DSL plus history discard on every request')
200K context vs rivals' 1M
SWE-Bench Pro claims didn't hold up in independent tests

models.proUsage: Approached with caution post-audit — pros who use it review output heavily rather than trust it on autonomous runs.

models.name: Llama 5
models.oneLiner: Meta's frontier-class open-weights model targeting GPT-5/Gemini-3 parity on reasoning, coding and agents.

sentiment.label: mixed
sentiment.summary: Keeps an open-weight option at the frontier, but practitioner buzz has shifted to Chinese labs — Llama is no longer the open-weight darling.
loved: Frontier-class open weights targeting GPT-5/Gemini-3 parity
gripes: Open-weight mindshare has decisively shifted to Kimi/MiniMax/DeepSeek
Llama Community License (700M MAU cap) is not fully permissive

weaknesses: Lost open-weight mindshare to Chinese labs
avoidFor: Apps over the 700M MAU license cap
Teams chasing the cheapest/best open value (Chinese models lead)
models.proUsage: Chosen mainly when a Western-lab open-weight model is required for governance; otherwise pros pick Chinese open models.

models.name: Mistral Large 3
models.oneLiner: Europe's leading open-weight frontier model — a 675B sparse MoE under Apache 2.0.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.summary: Europe's flagship open-weight MoE — the largest fully-permissive (Apache 2.0) model from a major Western lab, with vision.
loved: Largest open-weight MoE from a major Western lab
gripes: Predates the 2026 frontier wave (Dec 2025) and isn't topping current boards
256K context vs 1M-class peers

models.proUsage: The pick when a truly permissive (Apache 2.0) Western open-weight model is required for sovereign or commercial freedom.

models.name: Nemotron 3 Ultra 550B
models.oneLiner: NVIDIA's top open Nemotron tier — a 550B MoE under a fully permissive license, powerful but hard to self-host.

sentiment.label: mixed
sentiment.summary: Powerful and permissively licensed, but practically inaccessible — needs ~8 B200 GPUs to self-host and trails MiniMax M3 on intelligence.
loved: Most capable open model under a fully permissive license (OpenMDW-1.1)
Free variant also available on OpenRouter
gripes: Needs ~8 B200 GPUs to self-host (huge capex barrier)
Intelligence Index trails MiniMax M3
Enterprise-only positioning limits adoption

weaknesses: Trails leading open models on intelligence
avoidFor: Small teams without big GPU fleets
models.proUsage: Considered by enterprises with their own GPU fleets needing a permissively-licensed large open model on Bedrock/SageMaker.

models.name: Muse Spark
models.oneLiner: Meta Superintelligence Labs' first proprietary model — a strong multi-board debut and a strategic pivot from open source.

sentiment.label: mixed
sentiment.summary: A surprisingly strong proprietary debut (top-5 on LMArena Text and Vision on first appearance), but it marks Meta's controversial move away from open weights.
loved: #5 Text (1489) and #5 Vision (1295) on LMArena — top-5 debut
First model from Meta Superintelligence Labs
Deep integration with Meta's user data
gripes: Fully proprietary / no open weights — a pivot from Meta's open-source identity
Pricing/availability not yet on OpenRouter or AA

strengths: Backed by Meta's data and scale
models.proUsage: Mostly watched rather than deployed — strong benchmarks but limited external API access keeps it off most pro stacks for now.

models.name: Command A+
models.oneLiner: Cohere's sovereign/enterprise-focused open model — Apache 2.0, multimodal/agentic, with big agentic-benchmark gains.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.summary: A sovereign/enterprise-focused open model with strong agentic gains, efficient enough to run on ~2x H100.
loved: Big jumps on tau2-Bench Telecom (85%) and Terminal-Bench Hard (25%)
gripes: Not topping general leaderboards
Narrower enterprise/sovereign positioning
128K context vs 1M-class peers

models.proUsage: Chosen by enterprises needing a permissive, multilingual, efficiently-hostable agentic model for sovereign deployments.

upAndComing.name: MiniMax M3
upAndComing.why: Best intelligence-per-dollar on the AA leaderboard; #3 overall and #2 in coding on OpenRouter within a week of launch.

upAndComing.name: Kimi K2.6
upAndComing.why: The only non-Western model rated 'Tier-A with no caveats' — uniquely stable over 4,000+ tool calls / 13-hour agent runs.

upAndComing.name: Xiaomi MiMo-V2.5
upAndComing.why: #1 in the OpenRouter Programming category — a cheap multimodal open model owning coding-token volume at top value-per-dollar.
